message.abstract:
<jats:title>Summary</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In December 2019, pneumonia infected with a novel coronavirus burst in Wuhan, China. Now the situation is almost controlled in China but is worse outside China. We aimed to build a mathematical model to capture the global trend of epidemics outside China.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In this retrospective, outside-China diagnosis number reported from Jan 21 to Feb 28, 2020 was downloaded from WHO website. We develop a simple regression model on these numbers:
                    <jats:disp-formula id="ueqn1">
                      <jats:alternatives>
                        <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20029819v2_ueqn1.gif" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                      </jats:alternatives>
                    </jats:disp-formula>
                    where N
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    is the total diagnosed patient till the
                    <jats:italic>i</jats:italic>
                    th day, t=1 at Feb 1.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Based on this model, we estimate that there have been about 34 unobserved founder patients at the beginning of spread outside China. The global trend is approximately exponential, with the rate of 10 folds every 19 days.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Research in context</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Evidence before this study</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>In December 2019, pneumonia infected with a novel coronavirus burst in Wuhan, China. Now the situation is almost controlled in China but is worse outside China. Now there are 4,691 patients across 51 countries and territories outside China. We searched PubMed and the China National Knowledge Infrastructure database for articles published up to Feb 28, 2020, using the keywords “COVID”, “novel coronavirus”, “2019-nCoV” or “2019 novel coronavirus”. No published work about the global trend of epidemics outside China could be identified.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Added value of this study</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>We built a simple “log-plus” linear model to capture the global trend of epidemics outside China. We estimate that there have been about 34 unobserved founder patients at the beginning of spread outside China. The global trend is approximately exponential, with the rate of 10 folds every 19 days.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Implications of all the available evidence</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>With the limited number of data points and the complexity of the real situation, a straightforward model is expected to work better. Our model suggests that the COVID-19 disease follows an approximate exponential growth model stably at the very beginning. We predict that the number of confirmed patients outside China will increase ten folds in every 19 days without strong intervention by applying our model. Powerful actions on public health should be taken to combat this epidemic all over the world.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: COVID-19 Epidemic Outside China: 34 Founders and Exponential Growth

reference.unstructured: Wu JT , Leung K , Leung GM . Nowcasting and forecasting the potential domestic and international spread of the 2019-nCoV outbreak originating in Wuhan, China: A modeling study. Lancet. 2020;
Kim H. Outbreak of novel coronavirus (COVID-19): What is the role of radiologists? 2020.
Shao N , Pan H , Li X , et al. CoVID-19 in Japan: What could happen in the future? medRxiv, 2020.

Ryu S , Ali S T , Lim J , et al. The estimate of infected individuals of the 2019-Novel Coronavirus in South Korea by incoming international students from the countries of risk of 2019-Novel Coronavirus: a simulation study. medRxiv, 2020.
Zhao Q , Chen Y , Small D S. Analysis of the epidemic growth of the early 2019-nCoV outbreak using internationally confirmed cases. medRxiv, 2020.
Xiong H , Yan H. Simulating the infected population and spread trend of 2019-nCov under different policy by EIR model. Available at SSRN 3537083, 2020.
Mizumoto K , Kagaya K , Chowell G. Early epidemiological assessment of the transmission potential and virulence of 2019 Novel Coronavirus in Wuhan City: China, 2019-2020. medRxiv, 2020.

Zhao S. et al. Preliminary estimation of the basic reproduction number of novel coronavirus (2019-nCoV) in China, from 2019 to 2020: A data-driven analysis in the early phase of the outbreak. International Journal of Infectious Diseases, 2020.
